relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The human knew Eve, and life continued outside the garden. Eve received Cain as a child born with the Eternal still present in the human story.
Abel came as a breath-fragile brother, one who kept flocks, while Cain served the ground from which humanity had been formed.
In the passing of time, Cain brought an offering from the fruit of the ground.
Abel brought the first and rich portions of his flock, and the Eternal turned with regard toward Abel and his offering.
Cain felt the wound of not being received. Anger burned in him, and his face fell under the weight of comparison.
The Eternal met Cain at the doorway of his emotion and asked him to notice what was happening inside him.
The Divine warned him: if you align, you can rise; if not, the destructive pattern crouches at the door. It wants you, but you must master it.
Cain did not master the force at the doorway. In the field, brother rose against brother, and the first human blood was spilled.
The Eternal asked, Where is your brother? Cain answered from separation: Am I the keeper of my brother?
The Divine heard what Cain denied. Abel’s blood had a voice, and the ground itself cried out as witness.
Cain became cursed from the ground that had opened to receive his brother’s blood from his hand.
The ground would no longer yield strength to him as before. Cain became a fugitive and wanderer, displaced from rooted belonging.
Cain felt the consequence as unbearable, the weight of guilt and exile larger than he could carry.
He named his fear: hidden from the Divine face, cut off from the ground, exposed to the violence he had released.
Yet the Eternal placed a sign of protection on Cain, restraining revenge even after murder. Judgment did not become permission for endless violence.
Cain went out from the face of the Eternal and settled east of Eden, in the land of wandering.
Cain’s line began to build, seeking permanence in the city after exile from the garden.
Generations unfolded from Cain’s line, carrying the mixed inheritance of life, culture, distance, and unresolved violence.
Lamech took two women, and the distortion in relationship widened in the line of Cain.
From this line came tent-dwellers and keepers of livestock, the beginning of human livelihood and culture.
From this line also came music: string and pipe, beauty rising even from a wounded lineage.
From this line came metalwork and tools, the power to shape matter for creation or violence.
Lamech sang to his women, not in repentance but in boast. Violence had become a song of self-magnification.
What was once protection from revenge became in Lamech a fantasy of multiplied retaliation.
Life answered death through Seth, another seed appointed after Abel, showing that the line of restoration had not ended.
Through Seth came Enosh, the fragile mortal human; and then people began to call on the name of the Eternal.
